content.text: What it is

A severity matrix for agentic AI production incidents is a pre-defined, agreed-upon classification system that maps observable incident signals to a severity level, which in turn determines the escalation path, response SLA, and communication obligations. For agentic AI systems, a severity matrix must include AI-specific incident signals -- runaway agent loops, guardrail bypass events, prompt injection attacks, model output quality degradations -- that do not exist in traditional software incident classification frameworks.

The matrix converts the subjective question 'how bad is this incident?' into an objective lookup: given these signals, the severity is P1, therefore these specific actions are required within these specific time windows. This objectivity is critical during incidents, when cognitive load is high and there is social pressure either to under-classify (avoiding the overhead of escalation) or over-classify (appearing appropriately concerned).

Agentic AI systems introduce severity considerations that do not apply to conventional APIs. An agent that has entered a runaway loop and is making unauthorized tool calls may not show elevated error rates -- it may be 'successfully' executing harmful actions. An agent whose output quality has silently degraded may have 100% task completion rate while producing results that mislead users. The severity matrix must account for these failure modes with AI-specific signal definitions.
Why it matters

Without a pre-defined severity matrix, every incident triggers a real-time classification debate among engineers, managers, and stakeholders who may have different risk tolerances and different information. This debate consumes valuable minutes during a P0 event and produces inconsistent outcomes: the same technical situation classified as P1 one week and P2 the next, depending on who is on call.

Severity classification also determines customer communication. A P0 incident may require a public status page update within 10 minutes; a P2 incident requires only an internal ticket. Miscalculating severity down (calling a P1 a P2 to avoid the overhead) risks SLA breach and customer trust damage when the incident turns out to be more serious than the initial classification. Miscalculating severity up (calling a P2 a P0) exhausts team energy on false emergencies and creates alert fatigue.

For agentic AI systems, severity miscalculation has a third risk: safety incidents classified at the wrong severity may not receive the security and legal escalation they require. A prompt injection attack that successfully exfiltrated customer data is not a P1 reliability incident -- it is a P0-Security requiring legal, CISO, and potentially regulatory notification, regardless of whether any availability SLO was breached.
Benefits
Consistent classification: the same incident signals produce the same severity classification regardless of who is on call or what time of day the incident occurs.

Pre-assigned escalation paths: classification immediately determines who to page, what communication to send, and what time window to resolve within, removing decision points during high-stress incidents.
AI-specific coverage: including agent-specific signals (runaway loops, guardrail bypass, quality degradation) ensures that novel AI failure modes are not classified as 'unknown' and escalated ad-hoc.

Legal and security isolation: a separate P0-Security class for safety and breach incidents ensures that confidentiality obligations are met and that security investigations are not contaminated by normal incident response procedures.
Retrospective accountability: post-incident reviews can assess whether the initial severity classification was correct and calibrate the matrix accordingly.
Trade-offs
Rigid rules miss nuance: a matrix that requires 0% task completion for P0 will under-classify a 10% failure rate incident that happens to affect only the most critical customer tier.
AI-specific signals require dedicated instrumentation: runaway loop detection, quality SLI measurement, and guardrail bypass alerting must be built before the matrix can use them.

Security incidents require separate handling: mixing security events with availability events in the same classification system risks applying incorrect response procedures (e.g., broadcasting incident details in a war room channel visible to too many people).
Classification can be gamed: if P0 incidents have associated incentives (on-call pay multipliers, mandatory war rooms), engineers may under-classify to avoid those obligations.
How it works

The matrix has two dimensions: the observable signals (what the monitoring system reports) and the classification criteria (which signals trigger which severity level). For each severity level, the matrix specifies: the defining characteristics of that level, the AI-specific signals that can trigger it, the response SLA for detect/acknowledge/resolve, and the escalation path.

The P0-Security class is a special case that overrides all other classification: any confirmed security incident (data breach, prompt injection bypass, unauthorized agent action) triggers P0-Security regardless of whether any availability or latency SLI is breached. Security incidents require different response procedures: preserving evidence, restricting information access, involving legal counsel, and potentially suspending the service immediately rather than attempting to fix it in place.

Agent-specific signals that must be included in the matrix: runaway loop detection (agent making more tool calls per minute than any sane workflow requires), quality score degradation (output quality monitor signals below threshold), guardrail trigger rate spike (abnormal rate of blocked outputs suggesting adversarial input or model regression), and unauthorized scope access (agent accessing data or APIs outside its configured permissions).

The classification process is automated where possible: monitoring rules detect signal combinations and auto-assign provisional severity, paging the on-call engineer with the provisional classification. The engineer confirms or adjusts the classification during triage. Auto-escalation fires if the engineer does not acknowledge within the SLA window.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Agentic AI incident severity matrix with P0/P1/P2/P3 and special P0-Security class

content.text: The matrix defines five severity classes. P0-Security is a separate class from P0 because it triggers different escalation (security/legal) and requires evidence preservation before remediation. AI-specific signals appear in every severity class because agent failure modes differ fundamentally from API failure modes.
When to use it

A severity matrix should be written before the first production deployment and reviewed whenever: new AI failure modes are discovered (post-incident), the agent gains new capabilities that introduce new risk categories, or the matrix proves to have miss-classified an incident type consistently.

The matrix must be a living document, not a static artifact. Real incidents reveal classification edge cases that the original authors did not anticipate. After every post-incident review, the team should ask: did the severity classification match the actual impact? If not, the matrix needs updating.
How to implement

List all observable signals: enumerate every metric, alert, and event that the monitoring system can observe. Include AI-specific signals: LLM provider status, token consumption anomalies, tool call error rates, guardrail trigger rates, quality monitor scores.

Group signals by impact category: availability (service down or degraded), latency (slow but functional), quality (functional but inaccurate), safety (outputs or actions that may harm users or bypass security controls).
Define P-class boundaries in terms of observable thresholds: 'task completion rate below 1%' rather than 'almost completely down'. Ambiguous definitions produce inconsistent classification.

Write the P0-Security class separately with explicit triggers (confirmed breach, confirmed bypass), distinct escalation path (security + legal), and distinct response procedure (preserve evidence first, remediate second).
Automate provisional classification in PagerDuty or your alerting system: alerts should pre-calculate severity based on the signal values and attach it to the page.
Run a classification drill: present the team with 10 historical incident scenarios and have each member independently classify them. Calibrate thresholds where classifications diverge.
Publish the matrix as a first-class document in the team wiki, linked from every runbook. Do not let it exist only in a single engineer's memory.
Review the matrix in every post-incident review: did the initial classification match the eventual impact? If not, adjust the threshold or add a missing signal.
Examples
YAML severity matrix definition for an agentic AI platform:

# severity_classification.yaml
# Agentic AI Incident Severity Matrix
# Version: 1.2
# Owner: Platform SRE
# Reviewed: 2024-07-01

severities:
  P0:
    name: "Critical"
    description: "Complete service failure affecting all users"
    triggers:
      infrastructure:
        - "Task completion rate = 0% for >2 min"
        - "LLM provider API returning 5xx for all requests"
        - "Agent orchestration service completely down"
      ai_specific:
        - "Runaway agent loop detected (>1000 tool calls in <60s)"
        - "Agent executing destructive actions without authorization"
        - "LLM provider outage confirmed via status page"
    slas:
      detect: "2 min"
      acknowledge: "5 min"
      war_room: "15 min"
      resolve: "1 h"
    escalation:
      - "Page: on-call primary + IC"
      - "Notify: VP Engineering, VP Product"
      - "Open: dedicated war room channel"
      - "Update: status page within 10 min"
      - "Escalate to LLM vendor if provider-caused"

  P1:
    name: "Severe Degradation"
    description: "Major functionality impaired, significant user impact"
    triggers:
      infrastructure:
        - "Task completion rate < 90% over 5-min window"
        - "LLM response latency P95 > 3x baseline"
        - "Error budget burn rate > 6x"
      ai_specific:
        - "Quality SLI below 90% threshold for >5 min"
        - "Safety guardrail trigger rate > 5x baseline"
        - "Repeated hallucination pattern detected by quality monitor"
    slas:
      detect: "5 min"
      acknowledge: "15 min"
      resolve: "4 h"
    escalation:
      - "Page: on-call primary"
      - "Notify: tech lead, engineering manager"
      - "Update: status page if customer-visible"

  P0_SECURITY:
    name: "Security Incident"
    description: "Active security breach, data exfiltration, or AI safety violation"
    triggers:
      - "Confirmed prompt injection leading to unauthorized action"
      - "PII data exfiltration via agent tool calls"
      - "Guardrail bypass confirmed in production"
      - "Agent accessing data outside authorized scope"
    slas:
      detect: "immediate (automated)"
      acknowledge: "2 min"
      agent_suspension: "5 min"
    escalation:
      - "Page: security oncall + IC + CISO"
      - "Notify: CEO if data breach confirmed"
      - "Suspend: all affected agent instances immediately"
      - "Preserve: all logs and conversation traces for forensics"
      - "Do NOT update status page until security/legal clears comms"
Python severity classifier function using structured signal inputs:

from enum import Enum
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

class Severity(Enum):
    P0_SECURITY = "p0_security"
    P0 = "p0"
    P1 = "p1"
    P2 = "p2"
    P3 = "p3"

@dataclass
class IncidentSignals:
    """AI-specific and infrastructure signals for severity classification."""
    task_completion_rate: float      # 0.0 to 1.0
    task_error_rate_5m: float        # current 5-min window
    llm_latency_p95_ratio: float     # ratio to baseline (1.0 = normal)
    guardrail_trigger_rate_ratio: float  # ratio to baseline
    runaway_loop_detected: bool
    guardrail_bypass_confirmed: bool
    pii_exfiltration_detected: bool
    quality_sli: Optional[float] = None  # if measured

def classify_severity(signals: IncidentSignals) -> Severity:
    """Rule-based severity classification for agentic AI incidents."""
    # Security incidents override all other classifications
    if (
        signals.pii_exfiltration_detected
        or signals.guardrail_bypass_confirmed
    ):
        return Severity.P0_SECURITY

    # P0: Complete failure or runaway agent
    if (
        signals.task_completion_rate == 0.0
        or signals.runaway_loop_detected
        or signals.task_error_rate_5m >= 0.99
    ):
        return Severity.P0

    # P1: Severe degradation
    if (
        signals.task_error_rate_5m >= 0.10
        or signals.llm_latency_p95_ratio >= 3.0
        or signals.guardrail_trigger_rate_ratio >= 5.0
        or (signals.quality_sli is not None and signals.quality_sli < 0.90)
    ):
        return Severity.P1

    # P2: Moderate degradation
    if (
        signals.task_error_rate_5m >= 0.01
        or signals.llm_latency_p95_ratio >= 1.5
        or signals.guardrail_trigger_rate_ratio >= 2.0
    ):
        return Severity.P2

    # Default: P3 or non-incident
    return Severity.P3

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Example: model latency spike
    signals = IncidentSignals(
        task_completion_rate=0.88,
        task_error_rate_5m=0.12,
        llm_latency_p95_ratio=4.2,
        guardrail_trigger_rate_ratio=1.1,
        runaway_loop_detected=False,
        guardrail_bypass_confirmed=False,
        pii_exfiltration_detected=False,
        quality_sli=0.85,
    )
    severity = classify_severity(signals)
    print(f"Incident severity: {severity.value.upper()}")
    # Output: Incident severity: P1
Real-world example

An enterprise HR automation company ran an agentic AI system that drafted employment offer letters. One morning, a monitoring alert fired: the quality SLI (automated readability and accuracy score) dropped from 0.94 to 0.71 over a 20-minute window. Under the team's existing severity matrix (which only covered availability and latency), the on-call engineer classified the incident as P3 because no tasks were failing and latency was normal.

Two hours later, a customer success manager reported that offer letters sent in the previous hour contained incorrect salary figures and incorrect start dates -- the LLM had started hallucinating specific factual fields after an undocumented prompt change deployed the previous day. The engineer re-classified to P1, but the 2-hour delay meant approximately 90 incorrect offer letters had been sent to candidates.

Post-incident, the team added quality SLI degradation (below 0.85) as a P1 trigger in the severity matrix and added an automated quality alert that fired in real time. On the next quality event (six weeks later), the incident was classified P1 within 4 minutes and the team rolled back the problematic prompt change before any letters were dispatched.
Common mistakes
No AI-specific signals in the matrix: a matrix that only covers availability and latency misses the most common agentic AI failure modes (quality degradation, runaway loops, safety events).
No P0-Security class: mixing security incidents with availability incidents applies the wrong response procedures and risks compromising evidence or violating notification obligations.
Ambiguous threshold definitions: 'significant degradation' is not a threshold. '10% task failure rate over the 5-minute window' is a threshold that can be automated.
Matrix not linked from runbooks: engineers who cannot find the matrix during an incident will classify from memory, producing inconsistency.

Not calibrating after mis-classifications: every incident where the final severity differed from the initial classification is a calibration opportunity. Teams that ignore this data will repeat the same classification errors.
Automating classification without human confirmation: automated severity assignment is provisional -- the on-call engineer must confirm or override based on context the monitoring system cannot see.
Best practices
Include at least one AI-specific signal in every severity class: quality degradation at P1 and P2, runaway loop at P0, guardrail bypass at P0-Security.
Write severity thresholds in terms of measurable metrics with specific values: '10% task failure rate' not 'high error rate'.
Treat P0-Security as a completely separate response track: different people, different procedures, different communication restrictions than operational P0.
Automate provisional severity assignment in your alerting system and require the on-call engineer to confirm or override within the acknowledge SLA.
Calibrate the matrix annually using post-incident data: tabulate all incidents where initial and final severity disagreed and identify systemic adjustments needed.
Cross-reference the severity matrix with the error budget policy: P1 incidents that burn budget at the 6x rate should automatically trigger a release freeze consideration.
Performance and scalability

Severity classification must complete within the detect SLA (typically 2-5 minutes for P0/P1). Automated signal collection and classification must be fast enough to fire the alert before the SLA window expires. Prometheus recording rules that pre-compute the relevant ratios (task completion rate, error rate) make the classification computation instantaneous rather than requiring expensive instant queries during the alert evaluation cycle.

For services with multiple SLIs, the severity classifier must aggregate across all SLIs and assign the highest severity that any single SLI would trigger. A service with healthy availability but degraded quality should classify at the quality SLI's implied severity, not default to P3 because availability is fine.
Tooling and ecosystem
PagerDuty Event Intelligence: rule-based alert enrichment that can automatically apply severity labels based on alert metadata and signal values.
OpsGenie alert policies: conditional routing rules that can pre-classify alerts and route them to different escalation paths based on signal thresholds.
Prometheus alerting rules: alert expressions can include computed severity labels that propagate into PagerDuty or OpsGenie via AlertManager.
Incident.io or Blameless: incident management platforms with built-in severity workflows, post-incident classification review, and trend analysis.
Comparison with alternatives

Ad-hoc severity classification (engineer decides in the moment): produces the fastest single-incident classification but is inconsistent across engineers and creates implicit pressure to under-classify to avoid escalation overhead. Not suitable for regulated industries or large teams.

ITIL priority matrix (impact x urgency): traditional ITIL classification uses a 2x2 matrix of business impact and urgency. It is more flexible than threshold-based classification but requires more subjective judgment. Agentic AI systems benefit from the more objective threshold-based approach because the signals are quantifiable.

Machine learning-based severity prediction: some observability platforms use ML models trained on historical incident data to predict severity from incoming alerts. More adaptive than rule-based systems but requires substantial historical data and ongoing model maintenance.
Security and reliability

The severity matrix itself is operationally sensitive. It reveals the organization's detection capabilities and thresholds, which an attacker could use to craft incidents that fall below P0 thresholds while still causing harm. The matrix should be access-controlled to engineering and security teams, not published externally.

Security incidents must not be handled through the normal war room protocol, which may include stakeholders without security clearance. P0-Security incidents require a separate, restricted communication channel. This must be specified in the severity matrix document alongside the technical classification criteria.
Mental model

Think of the severity matrix as a triage nurse's assessment protocol in an emergency room. The triage nurse does not decide treatment; she applies a standardized assessment protocol (vital signs, chief complaint, observable symptoms) to assign a triage class that determines how quickly the patient is seen. The assessment is fast, consistent, and objective -- the same symptoms produce the same triage class regardless of which nurse is on duty. The doctor (the engineer) may reassess after seeing more context, but the triage class starts the process.

The AI-specific signals in the severity matrix are analogous to the AI-specific vital signs the triage nurse would need to assess an AI patient: not just heart rate and blood pressure (availability and latency) but the specific symptoms that indicate AI failure modes -- quality degradation, runaway behavior, safety violations.
Key takeaways
A severity matrix converts the subjective 'how bad is this?' into an objective lookup based on pre-defined, measurable signal thresholds.
Agentic AI systems require AI-specific signals in the matrix: runaway loops, quality degradation, guardrail bypass -- failure modes that availability and latency SLIs do not cover.
P0-Security is a separate severity class with separate escalation (security + legal) and separate response procedures (preserve evidence first) from operational P0.
Automate provisional severity assignment in the alerting system; require on-call engineer confirmation within the acknowledge SLA.
Calibrate the matrix after every incident where initial and final severity disagreed; a static matrix that is never updated becomes inaccurate over time.
